narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model looked at your academic record, LSAT score, family background, and other details to forecast your chances. For you specifically, it predicted a 68% probability that you would fail the bar exam initially, which is high enough that the university rejected your application to prioritize admits with stronger projected outcomes. The decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average of around 5.5—and this stood out as the strongest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, signaling potential challenges in sustaining strong performance later in your studies. Next in influence was your LSAT score of 40, which is actually above the average of 36.7 and worked in your favor, nudging the model toward expecting you to pass by demonstrating solid analytical skills. Your Year 1 decile of 5, roughly average compared to others' 5.7, had a milder but still negative pull, contributing somewhat to the failure prediction as it didn't show early standout achievement. Other aspects like your middle family income bracket and full-time student status aligned with the norm for most applicants (over 90% are full-time), so they didn't sway things much either way. Overall, while your LSAT was a bright spot that helped offset some concerns, the weaker Year 3 performance dominated, making your profile riskier than typical successful applicants who maintain higher deciles throughout. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law—many from similar backgrounds overcome these metrics—but the model prioritizes patterns from past bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it even higher, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted study or tutoring, and reapplying next cycle with updated transcripts that show upward momentum. You might also explore programs with holistic reviews or bar prep support tailored for underrepresented students like yourself. With focused effort on those academic trends, you can shift future predictions in your favor and get that admission. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
